jokes.text: Okay, ready? Now, first, you take off your clothes.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining how the TV taping works to the audience
jokes.punchline: Unexpected instruction to take off clothes
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection - sets up professional instruction then subverts with absurd directive. Clean structure, good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Okay, ready? Now, first, you take off your clothes.

jokes.text: Or does that say 'applesauce'?
jokes.setup: Larry pointing to applause signs
jokes.punchline: Misreading 'applause' as 'applesauce'
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay joke, functional but not particularly sharp or character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Or does that say 'applesauce'?

jokes.text: This tie? You like it? Doesn't make my butt look fat, does it?
jokes.setup: Larry asking about his tie
jokes.punchline: Asking if a tie makes his butt look fat
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Larry's vanity and neurosis - asking about a body part that has no relation to neckties. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: This tie? You like it? Doesn't make my butt look fat, does it?

jokes.text: I'm going, 'Oh, jeez. Well, at least he's off the island.'
jokes.setup: Story about seeing aged Gilligan in commercial
jokes.punchline: Silver lining is he finally escaped the island
jokes.explanation: Clever callback to Gilligan's Island premise with perfect comedic logic - finding absurd optimism in a depressing situation.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm going, 'Oh, jeez. Well, at least he's off the island.'

jokes.text: The joke doesn't work unless he ends with the words 'adjustable beds.'
jokes.setup: Watching Larry bomb a joke on stage
jokes.punchline: Technical critique of joke structure
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Artie's role as comedy veteran and his blunt assessment style. Meta-commentary on joke mechanics while character bombs on stage.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: The joke doesn't work unless he ends with the words 'adjustable beds.'

jokes.text: Toilet monitor is busted, boys.
jokes.setup: Artie and colleague heading to watch monitors by the toilet
jokes.punchline: Casual announcement that the bathroom TV is broken
jokes.explanation: Reveals the absurd reality of TV production - bathroom monitors are normal enough to have casual maintenance announcements.

dialogue.line: Toilet monitor is busted, boys.

jokes.text: She's halfway to her ideal weight, whatever that's supposed to mean.
jokes.setup: Asked about his wife Elaine
jokes.punchline: Confused dismissal of diet culture speak
jokes.explanation: Artie's masculine bewilderment at female diet terminology delivered with perfect gruff confusion.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: She's halfway to her ideal weight, whatever that's supposed to mean.

jokes.text: You've never been to the house before? Not that I recall. I have no mental picture.
jokes.setup: Larry inviting Artie over
jokes.punchline: Artie's blunt admission he's never been to Larry's house
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Larry's awkward realization and Artie's matter-of-fact response revealing their relationship boundaries.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You've never been to the house before?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Not that I recall. I have no mental picture.

jokes.text: Your House? What is that, a new Chinese place?
jokes.setup: Artie mentions dinner at 'your house'
jokes.punchline: Larry thinks it's a restaurant name
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection showing Larry's disconnection from normal social life - he literally can't process having people at his house.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Your House? What is that, a new Chinese place?

jokes.text: Be there or be square. I can do both.
jokes.setup: Larry using dated expression
jokes.punchline: Artie's literal interpretation
jokes.explanation: Quick wordplay on 'square' meaning both boring and geometric shape. Artie's dry delivery sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Be there or be square.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I can do both.

jokes.text: That's one person every two hours, half a person every hour.
jokes.setup: Larry calculating social obligation burden
jokes.punchline: Breaking down party guests into mathematical units
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry Sanders neurosis - treating social interaction like a mathematical problem to be optimized.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That's one person every two hours, half a person every hour.

jokes.text: They're half salt, half vodka. Swear to God, the olive floats at the top.
jokes.setup: Warning about Artie's drink-making
jokes.punchline: Absurd proportions causing olive to float
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy through description - the physics of Artie's terrible bartending creating floating olives.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: They're half salt, half vodka. Swear to God, the olive floats at the top.

jokes.text: Has anyone ever been to Larry's house for dinner? Not that I know of. That's weird. There must be a reason.
jokes.setup: Beverly mentioning the dinner invitation
jokes.punchline: Immediate suspicion that something is wrong
jokes.explanation: Establishes Larry's social isolation as office knowledge - his hospitality is so rare it's suspicious.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Has anyone ever been to Larry's house for dinner?
Not that I know of.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: That's weird. There must be a reason.

jokes.text: She's getting a promotion. Damn it!
jokes.setup: Friend trying to figure out why Larry invited Beverly
jokes.punchline: Sudden realization leading to immediate frustration
jokes.explanation: Simple reaction beat that works for character development. Nothing special but functional.

dialogue.speaker: Friend
dialogue.line: She's getting a promotion. Damn it!

jokes.text: These are vitamins.
jokes.setup: Hank claiming to have boxing tickets
jokes.punchline: Larry discovers they're just vitamin pills
jokes.explanation: Classic setup/payoff - Hank's desperate deception revealed through simple prop comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: These are vitamins.

jokes.text: Hey now! Well, I accept. I am... I'm very honored... and I would never miss a Larry Sanders event.
jokes.setup: Finally being invited to Larry's party
jokes.punchline: Hank's over-the-top formal acceptance speech for a simple dinner invitation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank character work - treating casual social invitation like major honor. Good escalation of formality.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Hey now! Well, I accept. I am... I'm very honored... and I would never miss a Larry Sanders event.

jokes.text: You tell her it's a big Larry Sanders shindig... a real time-of-your-life blowout. You tell her everybody's gonna be there.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to invite his date
jokes.punchline: Overselling intimate dinner as major event
jokes.explanation: Hank's tendency to oversell everything - turning small dinner into 'time-of-your-life blowout' shows his desperate insecurity.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You tell her it's a big Larry Sanders shindig... a real time-of-your-life blowout. You tell her everybody's gonna be there.

jokes.text: I wasn't invited. Hey. Hank'll make it right.
jokes.setup: Darlene mentioning she wasn't invited
jokes.punchline: Hank's confident promise despite having no authority
jokes.explanation: Hank's delusional confidence in his ability to solve problems he didn't create and has no power over.

dialogue.speaker: Darlene
dialogue.line: I wasn't invited.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Hey. Hank'll make it right.

jokes.text: As the talent booker, I work hands-on with the people. That's like a segment producer. To be blunt, she's like a glorified secretary.
jokes.setup: Jeannie pleading her case for promotion
jokes.punchline: Dismissive characterization of Beverly's work
jokes.explanation: Office politics desperation - Jeannie's professional jealousy leads to catty dismissal of colleague.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: As the talent booker, I work hands-on with the people. That's like a segment producer. To be blunt, she's like a glorified secretary.

jokes.text: Oh, shit. That fuckin' dinner. For once I try and join my personal life with my work life.
jokes.setup: Larry realizing dinner caused office drama
jokes.punchline: Regret at attempting normal social behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry Sanders moment - his one attempt at normal human connection immediately backfires into workplace drama.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, shit. That fuckin' dinner. For once I try and join my personal life with my work life.

jokes.text: I'm gonna bring my tarot cards tonight and do your wife.
jokes.setup: Darlene planning party entertainment
jokes.punchline: Unintentional sexual innuendo
jokes.explanation: Basic double entendre humor - relies on accidental sexual meaning rather than clever writing.

dialogue.speaker: Darlene
dialogue.line: I'm gonna bring my tarot cards tonight and do your wife.

jokes.text: But just the tarot reading will be fine.
jokes.setup: Darlene's unintended innuendo about 'doing' his wife

jokes.explanation: Larry's uncomfortable response to the innuendo - his awkward attempt to clarify without acknowledging the sexual meaning.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: But just the tarot reading will be fine.

jokes.text: Oh, Larry! What should I bring tonight? Ice? Chips? Salt!
jokes.setup: Phil asking what to bring to the party
jokes.punchline: Escalating from normal party items to random condiment

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Oh, Larry! What should I bring tonight? Ice? Chips? Salt!

jokes.text: Phil, you know, I've got this... Ulcer? Party.

jokes.punchline: Phil assumes it's a medical problem
jokes.explanation: Quick misdirection showing how Larry's hesitant delivery makes Phil assume bad news.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Phil, you know, I've got this... Ulcer? Party.

jokes.text: You're putting everything we own into a box. Just a couple of things, like Aquarium Digest.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Just a couple of things, like Aquarium Digest.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Then they'll decide we're not as happy as Burt Reynolds and Loni.

jokes.text: You do drink. I do not drink.

jokes.explanation: Larry's reflexive denial of obvious facts when under social pressure - basic but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: You do drink.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I do not drink.

jokes.text: Ooh, you're not my beautiful wife. Hey, hey, welcome. No dates?

dialogue.speaker: Larry

jokes.text: It is! You cry every time at the end... when he finishes with his mission on Earth and has to go back to heaven.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie
dialogue.line: It is! You cry every time at the end... when he finishes with his mission on Earth and has to go back to heaven.

dialogue.line: Just because Cheers was a hit, every asshole thinks he can mix a drink.

Cost him a few thousand dollars too.
A few thousand dollars?

It's not that funny.

dialogue.speaker: Hank

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie

dialogue.speaker: Larry

dialogue.speaker: Martin

dialogue.speaker: Martin

dialogue.speaker: Martin

jokes.punchline: Euphemistic reference to sexual activity

jokes.text: Five marriages.

dialogue.line: Five marriages.

jokes.explanation: Larry deflecting by attacking his competition - 'woman who claims to be his wife' is perfect catty showbiz gossip.

jokes.text: Great party. Great. It was great.
